name: Charlwood
coordsSource: web-verified:wikipedia-os-grid SU67943-30856;ropley-1839-tithe-map charlwood-lane-junction;cross-check adjacent monkwood SU667309; 2026-06-05
overview: Charlwood is a small rural hamlet in the civil parish of Ropley (and partly East Tisted) in East Hampshire, lying within the South Downs National Park between Monkwood and the A31. It is first recorded in 1218 as Cherlewde, meaning the wood of the free peasant, and its oldest surviving building, Charlwood House, appears on a 1635 estate map of the Rotherfield Park area. Today it is a dispersed scatter of period houses, cottages and farmland with no commercial centre of its own, sitting roughly between the Georgian town of New Alresford and Alton. Like the wider parish of Ropley, it is characterised by an almost total absence of pavements, in favour of boundary walls, hedges and mature trees.
character: Quiet, leafy and distinctly agricultural - a dispersed cluster of country houses and cottages set among woods and paddocks rather than a nucleated village.
amenities: No shop, pub or school within the hamlet itself
Village shop and Ropley CofE Primary in nearby Ropley (~2 miles)
Co-op convenience store and further shops at Four Marks (~2 miles)
Full shops, cafes, a deli, Co-op and Tesco Express in New Alresford (~10-15 min drive)
Extensive footpaths and bridleways within the South Downs National Park

commutes.notes: Nearest town for shops, supermarkets and services
Nearest mainline station; South Western Railway to London Waterloo
Cathedral city with fast trains to London Waterloo
From Alton station, about 15 min drive away

prices.summary: Mostly detached houses and period cottages on large plots; sold prices in the surrounding Ropley, Monkwood and Selborne lanes typically run from around 550,000 pounds to well over 1m, with occasional multi-million-pound country houses, reflecting the South Downs National Park setting.

thingsToDo: Ride the Watercress Line (Mid-Hants Railway) heritage steam trains from Ropley and New Alresford
Walk the St Swithun's Way / Pilgrims' Way, which runs through Ropley
Visit Gilbert White's House & Gardens and National Trust Selborne Common at nearby Selborne
Walk or cycle the footpaths and bridleways of the South Downs National Park
placesToEat: Pubs, cafes and a deli in the Georgian town of New Alresford (~10-15 min drive)
Village shop in Ropley for everyday supplies
pros: Highly scenic, unspoilt setting within the South Downs National Park
Period houses and cottages on generous plots; very rural and quiet
Well-regarded primary (Ropley CofE) and secondary (Perins) catchment
Easy reach of Georgian New Alresford and the A31 towards Alton and Winchester
cons: No shop, pub or school in the hamlet itself - a car is essential
Nearest mainline station is Alton, about 15 minutes' drive (the local Watercress Line is heritage-only)
Limited stock and high prices; few homes change hands
whoItSuits: Buyers wanting a quiet, high-amenity countryside base inside the South Downs National Park, who are content to drive to Alresford, Alton or Winchester for shops, schools and trains.
